1→---
     2→title: "Introduction - Information Technology"
     3→part: 21
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3507-3518"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY**
     9→
    10→**Role of Information Technology in a corporate**  
    11→[Information technology (IT) plays a pivotal role in modern corporates by enhan-cing efficiency, productivity, and decision making.](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=69e63a19112b524d4b61569a10ee63dd1a9e27dee7c96065d51860f2aa915786JmltdHM9MTc2OTQ3MjAwMA&ptn=3&ver=2&hsh=4&fclid=1ea294ad-f45c-620f-3040-8243f53e6335&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly9uZXdob3Jpem9uY29sbGVnZW9mZW5naW5lZXJpbmcuaW4vcm9sZS1vZi1pbmZvcm1hdGlvbi10ZWNobm9sb2d5LWluLWJ1c2luZXNzLw&ntb=1)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
    12→
    13→[IT systems automate routine tasks, streamline workflows, and enable data analytics, leading to improved business outcomes. IT also facilitates better ](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=69e63a19112b524d4b61569a10ee63dd1a9e27dee7c96065d51860f2aa915786JmltdHM9MTc2OTQ3MjAwMA&ptn=3&ver=2&hsh=4&fclid=1ea294ad-f45c-620f-3040-8243f53e6335&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly9uZXdob3Jpem9uY29sbGVnZW9mZW5naW5lZXJpbmcuaW4vcm9sZS1vZi1pbmZvcm1hdGlvbi10ZWNobm9sb2d5LWluLWJ1c2luZXNzLw&ntb=1)  
    14→[communication, customer engagement, and security, making it an indispen-sable part of any business's operations.](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=69e63a19112b524d4b61569a10ee63dd1a9e27dee7c96065d51860f2aa915786JmltdHM9MTc2OTQ3MjAwMA&ptn=3&ver=2&hsh=4&fclid=1ea294ad-f45c-620f-3040-8243f53e6335&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly9uZXdob3Jpem9uY29sbGVnZW9mZW5naW5lZXJpbmcuaW4vcm9sZS1vZi1pbmZvcm1hdGlvbi10ZWNobm9sb2d5LWluLWJ1c2luZXNzLw&ntb=1)
    15→
    16→IT is a service provider to the employees of an organisation to facilitate conducts their business. It includes providing hardware like network systems, computers and laptops and services like the internet, wifi, 5G in mobiles, email system, communication systems, Video and Audio conferencing systems, and also providing various kinds of software required by different employees for their work. They also integrate hardware, software and services together and also maintain the systems and create a 99% plus uptime. They also provide makeshift arrangements during the unfortunate downtime moments.
    17→
    18→Without technology, it’s not only difficult to perform but impossible to do so. Therefore IT is a huge enabler to the performance of an organisation. So, it has to create solid security measures to prevent cyber-attacks in different forms to safeguard their hardware and software, not only to ensure safety of the systems but also to protect against data security.
    19→

1→---
     2→title: "LOW-TECH to LOW-LEVEL"
     3→part: 21
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3519-3534"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : LOW-TECH to LOW-LEVEL**
     9→
    10→Many leaders of older generation, by virtue of occupying senior positions in corporates, are provided high end computers, laptops and tablets for their performance. Many such leaders do not know to use them properly except somehow being able to log in to their mail account. Such trevial jobs could have been performed easily by a low end laptop but due to seniority, prestige and ego, the IT Head provides him a very costly laptop underutilized forever.
    11→
    12→And it could be the vice-versa. I have seen many junior people handling data crunching graphics like Autocad drawings being given very low end computers or laptops which struggle to display the complex diagrams on screen for minutes. The poor chap, being junior with no ego, deserves a high end laptop but cannot get it as per the IT rules of the company. What a misery\!
    13→
    14→What can the IT Head do? Firstly it should find out the configuration of a system required for different applications. In the above case, the draughtsman working with the Autocad software must get a high end laptop. After all, the company gains, isn’t it? For the higher positions requiring mundane jobs to be performed by him on the laptop, the boss may opt for a lower end laptop or no laptop at all (in case he has his personal one) against a financial incentive like a Manager getting car allowance in case he does not opt to take a car from the company even though he is eligible for the same. 
    15→
    16→Therefore why not provide incentive to a senior leader for not taking a laptop? The company may incur a much lower cost than providing the laptop. 
    17→
    18→**The salient points are:**
    19→
    20→1. The configuration of a laptop or computer should be the basis of providing the same to the employees based on the type of software the employee uses.  
    21→2. It is pointless to provide high-end laptops to seniors who would use low-end software.  
    22→3. Incentive may be given to those who would not opt for a laptop and use his own. However due to security reasons, the organization may not allow employees to use their own laptops.
    23→

1→---
     2→title: "TOO LATE UP-GRADATION"
     3→part: 21
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3535-3547"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : TOO LATE UP-GRADATION**
     9→
    10→We all know about the fast paced advancements in hardware and software industry today. So an organisation must keep a budget to modify, upgrade or procure new hardware and software as time changes. Often I find that IT in order to save money continues providing and maintaining the old systems until the people give up using them and thereafter they upgrade to newer systems. The IT Head thinks that he has saved costs for the company, but in hindsight, he has escalated cost. Here are a few points to illustrate this.
    11→
    12→1\) With the prolonged use of the old systems, the new systems procured at a later stage costs more and also the sale value of the old systems become lesser. So cost rises on both accounts.
    13→
    14→2\) As the old systems were used for a prolonged period, the productivity of various personnel reduces, more so because the down time of the systems increase due to ageing systems.
    15→
    16→**The salient points are:**
    17→
    18→1. Hardware and software should be upgraded from time to time and purchased when outdated so that employees can reap the benefits of the latest hardware and software.   
    19→2. It is pointless to wait and continue using old systems and software because in the long run the organization will lose out on the benefits of using new systems and latest software.
    20→

1→---
     2→title: "Some Good Practices"
     3→part: 21
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3548-3561"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**
     9→
    10→**Requirement of software**  
    11→IT should enquire with different departments of the organization on their requirement of specialized software so as to organize and streamline their processes and date with ease.
    12→
    13→**Provide laptops to juniors if required**  
    14→Sometimes, some juniors often need to work at home in urgent situations, given their type of job. Those juniors should not be denied laptops stating that their positions do not allow to be given laptops. This is a serious lapse in IT policies of organizations. Sometimes employees, who require laptops are not given and some who do not require are given, just based on their senior positions. Actually there is a tag of prestige with people who are given laptops irrespective of whether they require or not.
    15→
    16→**Timely Backups**  
    17→Sometimes IT managers ignore timely backups assuming cloud storage has it all. Timely backups are essential other than cloud storage 
    18→
    19→**Lack of cyber security training**  
    20→Often lack of cyber security training leads to unknown cyber threats to employees.
    21→

1→---
     2→title: "Introduction - Learning & Development"
     3→part: 22
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3564-3580"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**L\&D (LEARNING & DEVELOPMENT)**
     9→
    10→**Role of L\&D in a corporate**  
    11→The role of Learning and Development is multifold
    12→
    13→1) Enhancing skills – identifying skill gaps and customising skill training  
    14→2) Career development – with skill training, employees develop their career  
    15→3) Leadership training – potential employees are given leadership training to become the leaders of future  
    16→4) Onboarding recruits – New recruits are provided all round training about the organization  
    17→5) Compliance training – employees are made aware on the relevant laws and regulations 
    18→
    19→The only “constant” on this earth is “change”. Everything changes with time; the climate, environment, technology, research, theories, practice, management techniques and also the people. We are going through an evolution of everything at a fast pace. And therefore we need to change the way we work. But how do we change? Do we at all know what to change?
    20→
    21→If we don’t know or know what to change but don’t know how to change, then training & learning is the only way to catch up with the latest modern methods. Therefore L\&D is an important department in an organization to keep their employees abreast with times of change. Without L\&D it is difficult for an organisation to keep up with the latest technologies or management that is required to compete in the market.
    22→
    23→L\&D may outsource lecturers or professional practitioners in different subjects to address different groups of people as per their different learning needs. Or else it can also depute existing employees with great skills and knowledge to impart training to juniors and others. Now a days, all corporates have a L\&D budget so that they can outsource good expertise for the training of their employees.
    24→

1→---
     2→title: "NO REGULAR FEEDBACK ON TRAINING"
     3→part: 22
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3581-3594"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : NO REGULAR FEEDBACK ON TRAINING**
     9→
    10→L\&D prepares an annual training chart for most employees in their areas of interest and requirements. After a training session which may typically last from one day to seven days, L\&D distributes a form to the participants asking for feedback and ends the training session.
    11→
    12→But from hereon is the issue under question. Typically it has been found that L\&D does not monitor the implementation of such learnings by the employees post the training session. Even if they ask some employees subsequently on the implementation of their learnings, they may get positive or negative reports. These reports are not compiled in a structured manner. Thus the L\&D has no idea on the benefits of such training despite spending considerable money.  
    13→However they are happy that one of their KRAs required for their annual appraisals have been fulfilled. What non sense\!
    14→
    15→L\&D must device mechanisms to take a structured feedback from all employees for the next one year at different time intervals to understand as to how the learning was implemented in different spheres of work by the employees. It’s so easy, yet not done because the fulfillment of the KRA is in their minds, not the gains that the organisation should have made\!
    16→
    17→**The salient points are:**
    18→
    19→1. L\&D must conduct training sessions to their employees as per requirements to update knowledge and keep abreast of changing times.  
    20→2. L\&D must not focus on their KRA fulfillment, but establish methods to receive feedback from employees.
    21→

1→---
     2→title: "GAINS NOT ESTIMATED"
     3→part: 22
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3595-3607"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : GAINS NOT ESTIMATED**
     9→
    10→Not only is the above absence of feedback under question, the benefits too are doubtful. What is more significant is that L\&D, with the reports of feedback at regular time intervals, should also discuss with each employee on the optimisation of the time, technology, management and cost that the employee has been able to benefit due to the training. These benefits should be recorded against each employee and the summation would then be the benefit of the organization.  
    11→   
    12→It is obviously understood that the benefit to the organization from conducting the training courses should far surpass the expenses incurred. After all, an organization is not conducting training sessions for charity\! However, if the benefits gained are not significant, then there is a question either on the choice of the training itself or in its implementation by the employees. 
    13→
    14→Here comes the concept of Training productivity which is equal to Total estimated benefits/Cost of training. The ratio will be a measure of the productivity due to trainings organised by L\&D.
    15→
    16→**The salient points are:**
    17→
    18→1. L\&D must keep an account for the estimated benefits arisen against each employee or a group of people to arrive at the total estimated benefit of the organization.  
    19→2. Training productivity \= Total estimated benefits/Cost of training to be measured annually for the L\&D department.
    20→

1→---
     2→title: "TOO MANY IN THE LAST QUARTER"
     3→part: 22
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3608-3626"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : TOO MANY IN THE LAST QUARTER**
     9→
    10→In an cricket ODI, if you play casually without targets in the beginning and keep too many runs to be scored in the last overs, then you know what happens? Even if you sometimes win in such circumstances, the batters are stressed to extremes in the last few overs.
    11→
    12→Similar is the case with L\&D. If you take a casual approach in the beginning of a year conducting only a few training sessions, then you keep too many to be conducted in the last quarter to fulfill your KRA targets. This situation leads to many complexities.
    13→
    14→1\. Trainees will not be able to grasp so many subjects in a short period of time due to so many training sessions and they will feel stressed.  
    15→2\. Trainees will lack interest to attend so many sessions in the last quarter since they will also be hard pressed to meet their annual operational targets.  
    16→3\. Trainees, even if they attend most of such programs in the last moment, will not be able to achieve their corresponding benefits hurriedly. Therefore the Training Productivity will fall.  
    17→4\. Sometimes, some trainees will drop out because of last quarter work pressure and therefore the class will comprise of very few trainees which will reduce enthusiasm of the lecturers to train.
    18→
    19→Therefore L\&D must ensure that training programs are organised every month and do not keep most of them for the last quarter of the financial year.
    20→
    21→**The salient points of are:**
    22→
    23→1. L\&D must organise their training sessions uniformly over all the quarters of the financial year.  
    24→2. L\&D must be careful to hold only few training sessions during the last quarter of the year.  
    25→3. L\&D must also ensure that training sessions are taken by employees across the organization.
    26→

1→---
     2→title: "Some Good Practices"
     3→part: 22
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3627-3640"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**
     9→
    10→**Cost-benefit analysis**  
    11→L\&D department needs to discuss with each departmental employees about their training requirements. Once trained, they need to also analyse the benefits that would accrue to an organization compared to present day situation. Training productivity must be measured annually.  
    12→   
    13→**Internal faculty**  
    14→Sometimes organizations ignore talent of their senior employees or are not able to get time from their senior employees to impart training in a particular subject of his expertise. Instead the L\&D depends on hiring outside professionals incurring more cost. Outside faculty is definitely important to get a 360 degree aspect of the subject or when such expertise is not available within the organization. But involving internal faculty can save lot of money for the organization.
    15→
    16→**Lack of alignment with the vision of organization**  
    17→Some L\&D programs are not aligned with the objectives and goals of the organisation. Such irrelevant training do not produce desired results
    18→
    19→**L\&D not in pace with changing times**  
    20→The world is moving in a fast pace with new technologies, methods, new software and hardware. L\&D must provide training in new evolving areas at a fast pace which are relevant to the organisation
    21→

1→---
     2→title: "Introduction - Insurance"
     3→part: 23
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3643-3655"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**INSURANCE**  
     9→	  
    10→**Role of Insurance in a corporate**  
    11→Insurance plays an important role in an organization by protecting against various risks that could disrupt business operations. Insurance ensures financial stability while mitigating risks. Comprehensive insurance policies like life and health insurance also attracts talent. Some legal compliance like mandatory third party insurance for cars is met by taking insurances.
    12→
    13→You must be aware of Car Insurance. If you are an owner of a bike or a car, you know that car insurance is mandatory if you own a car or a bike. The insurance can be a Third  party insurance covering loss or damage to third parties which is mandatory while comprehensive insurance which covers all the cars for bumper to bumper damage and all the individuals involved in the accident including yourself is optional in India. However, Personal Accident Insurance, known as PA cover, is also mandatory as per law in India.
    14→
    15→We also have house insurance covering loss or damage to house property and its contents, life insurance covering financial benefits to your nominee in case of your death, health insurance covering medical expenses of yourself and your family, EMI insurance covering waiver of EMIs in case of your death, cybercrime insurance in case you are cheated or extorted by cyber criminals.  
    16→	  
    17→You may observe that all the above kinds of insurances are covers to different risks. And the premium is a cost incurred to cover the risk. 
    18→
    19→Similar to above insurances, you can also take a Project insurance covering the losses or damage to a Project due to unforeseen circumstances. Coupled with this, you have to take a Workmen Compensation Policy (WCP) to cover lives of workmen and vehicle insurances for all the vehicles attached to the project or elsewhere. WCP has to be mandatorily taken for any project in India.
    20→

1→---
     2→title: "FALSE CLAIMS"
     3→part: 23
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3656-3667"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : FALSE CLAIMS**
     9→
    10→Some managers in the Insurance department tend to claim higher amounts for project losses than actually the losses are. They feel that the surveyor from the insurance company will find out some discrepancies and strike some portion of their claims, hence claim higher. In my opinion, such an act is unethical. And if the claims are genuine, then it is also unethical on the part of the surveyor to strike some portion of the claims. I have observed that employees unethically submit higher claims in fear and anticipation that some portion of the claims will be struck off by the surveyor.  
    11→
    12→Such an unfair practice on the part of the employee as well as on the part of the surveyor is unwarranted. Employees should resort to filing correct claims with proof and surveyors should okay claims wherever they are substantiated with proof.
    13→
    14→**The salient points of are:**
    15→
    16→1. Insurance department should not resort to false claims of higher amounts than the actual losses.  
    17→2. Insurance department should convince the surveyors that their claims are genuine and should not be opposed.
    18→

1→---
     2→title: "NO TARGETS"
     3→part: 23
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3668-3681"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : NO TARGETS**
     9→
    10→I have observed that the Insurance manager has several claims filed for different projects in the past but have no time targets to receive them. New documents as required from insurance companies from time to time are submitted as and when required but there is no hurry for the Insurance Manager to receive the claims. This is very astonishing.
    11→
    12→Insurance Managers must be sensitised that claims filed are because the project have undergone losses, yet the Manager has no concern. Sometimes it may happen that the claims are itself the profits for the project, in other words, the project will incur losses if the claims are not received. 
    13→
    14→Therefore, every claim must have a set time target and the performance of the manager has to be evaluated with respect to fulfilling these time targets. Losses of a project or diminishing profits must be arrested through these claims. The claims received shall be far more in value than paying salaries for a dedicated manager for the same. Hence claims claim an important parameter in project profitability.
    15→
    16→**The salient points of are:**
    17→
    18→1. Insurance claims must be filed promptly as soon as losses or theft is reported from the site.  
    19→2. Every insurance claim must have a time target and the Insurance Manager be appraised accordingly  
    20→3. Receipt of insurance claims can offset the losses of a project or improve its profitability.
    21→

1→---
     2→title: "Some Good Practices"
     3→part: 23
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3682-3697"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**
     9→
    10→**Listing progress of Insurance claims**  
    11→Receiving insurance claims is a lengthy process. Documentary proof is the essential part in claiming insurances. Insurance claims may pertain to different projects that an organization is executing. Also, there may be multiple insurance claims against the same project. Again, many claims may have been filed with different insurance agencies. The Insurance Manager must be very active to interact with all the agencies, submit further documents as and when required and expedite claims for the organization.
    12→
    13→Remember that the loss against which the insurances have been claimed is already incurred by the organization. Therefore to replenish those costs as soon as possible is of prime importance. The claims received jacks up the profit of the project which had depleted due to the loss incurred. The performance of the Insurance Manager will also take into account of the time that is required by the Manager to receive the claims.
    14→
    15→**Choosing the cheapest policy**  
    16→Cheapest policy may not cover the major business risks of an organization. Just do not choose a cheap insurance just because you are mandated to get one.
    17→
    18→**Update insurance**  
    19→As business grows, the financial and technical dimensions too grow in different directions. You may need to update the terms of insurance or change your insurance plan to cover the current risks of the organisation.  
    20→   
    21→**Annual insurance review**  
    22→Review all insurance policies annually and update or change insurance policies with changing and increasing risks. Consult industry specialists if required
    23→

1→---
     2→title: "Introduction - Ethics"
     3→part: 24
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3700-3712"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**ETHICS**
     9→
    10→**Role of Ethics in a corporate**  
    11→Ethics facilitate compliance, trust and sustainable growth. Trust is essential to retain employees, customers, vendors, in fact all the stakeholders. Trust attracts talent and increases investor confidence. Ethical standards help to maintain the culture of the corporate and facilitate leaders to take proper actions against complex unethical issues.  
    12→
    13→Though ethical standards are not mandatory by any all, it helps in compliance to industry regulations and expectations of the society. It also contributes to sustainability, welfare of the society and environmental responsibility. 
    14→
    15→Today, Ethics has become an integral part of doing business. Ethics is basically doing a job honestly with the right practices as the organization, industry and the society demands.     32→A **sustainable business**, or a green business, is an enterprise which has (or aims to have) a minimal negative impact or potentially a positive effect on the global or local [environment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_environment), [community](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Community), society, or economy—a [busi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Business)ness that attempts to meet the [triple bottom line](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Triple_bottom_line). They cluster under different groupings and the whole is sometimes referred to as "[green capitalism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Green_capitalism)". Often, sustainable businesses have progressive environmental and human rights, policies. In general, a business described as green if it matches the following four criteria:
    33→
    34→1. It incorporates principles of sustainability into each of its business decisions.  
    35→2.  It supplies environmentally friendly product or services that replace demand for non-green products and/or services.  
    36→3.  It is greener than traditional competition.  
    37→4.  It has made an enduring commitment to environmental principles in its business operations.
    38→
    39→The Brundtland Report emphasized that sustainability is a three-legged stool of people, planet, and profit. Sustainable businesses within the supply chain try to balance all three using sustainable development and sustainable distribution to affect the environment, business growth, and society.
    40→
    41→A major initiative of sustainable businesses is to eliminate or decrease the environmental harm caused by the production and consumption of their goods. The impact of such human activities in terms of the number of greenhouse gases produced can be measured in units of carbon dioxide and is referred to as the *carbon footprint*. The carbon footprint concept is derived from the ecological footprint analysis, which examines the ecological capacity required to support the consumption of products
    42→
    43→In the environmental sphere, sustainable practices include going paperless, refurbishing used products, *revising production processes* to eliminate waste, up cycling clothes or textiles. In the social sphere, for a business to be truly sustainable, it must sustain not only the necessary environmental resources, but also social resources including employees, customers (the community), and its reputation.
    44→
    45→Green washing refers to make vague or false environmental claims about a product or service they are selling. Green washing also refers to an act of over exaggerating the beneficial effects a product may have on the environment
    46→
    47→**The widely practiced strategies include Innovation, Collaboration, Process Improvement, Sustainability reporting and greening the supply chain.**
    48→
    49→1.  Innovation & Technology: This method focuses on a company's ability to change its products and services towards better environmental impacts, for example less waste production.
    50→
    51→2.  Collaboration: The formation of networks with similar or partner companies facilitates knowledge sharing and propels innovation.
    52→
    53→3.  Process Improvement: Continuous process surveying and improvement are essential to reduction in negative impacts. Employee awareness of company-wide sustainability plan further aids the integration of new and improved processes.
    54→
    55→4.  Sustainability Reporting: Periodic reporting of company performance in relation to goals encourages performance monitoring internally and transparency and accountability externally. The goals might then be incorporated into the corporate mission.
    56→
    57→5.  Greening the Supply Chain: Sustainable procurement is important for any sustainability strategy as a company's impact on the environment is much bigger than the products that they consume. The B Corporation (certification) model is a good example of one that encourages companies to focus on this.
    58→
    59→One critical milestone was the establishment of the ISO 14000 standards whose development came as a result of the Rio Summit on the Environment held in 1992\. This standard is the cornerstone standard of the ISO 14000 series. This specifies a framework of control for an Environmental Management System against which an organization can be certified by a third party.

    10→**Role of CSR in a corporate**  
    11→Corporate social responsibility (CSR) is a [self-regulating business model](https://www.investopedia.com/b-corp-7488828) that helps a company be socially accountable to itself, its stakeholders, and the public. By practicing corporate social responsibility, also called [corporate citizenship](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/c/corporatecitizenship.asp), companies are aware of how they impact aspects of society, including economic, social, and environmental. Engaging in CSR means a company operates in ways that [enhance society and the environment](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/c/conscious-capitalism.asp) instead of contributing negatively to them.
    12→
    13→Through [corporate social responsibility programs](https://www.investopedia.com/ask/answers/011215/how-did-johnson-and-johnsons-corporate-responsibility-policy-pay-1982.asp), philanthropy, and volunteer efforts, businesses can benefit society while boosting their brands. A [socially responsible](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/s/social-audit.asp) company is accountable to itself and its shareholders. CSR is commonly a strategy employed by large corporations. The more visible and successful a corporation is, the more responsibility it has to set standards of ethical behavior for its peers, competition, and [industry](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/i/industry.asp).
    14→
    15→**Types of CSR**  
    16→**Environmental responsibility**: Corporate social responsibility is rooted in preserving the environment. A company can pursue environmental stewardship by reducing pollution and emissions in manufacturing, recycling materials, replenishing natural resources like trees, or creating product lines consistent with CSR.
    17→
    18→**Ethical responsibility**: Corporate social responsibility includes acting fairly and ethically. Instances of ethical responsibility include fair treatment of all [customers](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/c/customer.asp) regardless of age, race, culture, or sexual orientation, favorable pay and benefits for employees, [vendor](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/v/vendor.asp) use across demographics, full disclosures, and transparency for investors.
    19→
    20→**Philanthropic responsibility**: CSR requires a company to contribute to society, whether a company donates profit to charities, enters into [transactions](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/t/transaction.asp) only with suppliers or vendors that align with the company philanthropically, supports employee philanthropic endeavors, or sponsors fundraising events.  
    21→**Financial responsibility**: A company might make plans to be more environmentally, ethically, and philanthropically focused, however, it must back these plans through financial investments in programs, donations, or product research including [research and development](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/r/randd.asp) for products that encourage sustainability, creating a diverse workforce, or implementing DEI, social awareness, or environmental initiatives.
    22→
    23→In 2010, the [International Organization for Standardization (ISO)](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/i/international-organization-for-standardization-iso.asp) released ISO 26000, a set of voluntary standards to help companies implement corporate social responsibility. Unlike other ISO standards, ISO 26000 provides guidance rather than requirements because the nature of CSR is more qualitative than quantitative, and its standards cannot be certified. ISO 26000 clarifies social responsibility and helps organizations translate CSR principles into practical actions.
    24→
    25→Companies striving to [measure success beyond bottom-line financial results](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/f/ftse4good-index.asp) may adopt CSR strategies that target environmental, ethical, philanthropic, and fiscal responsibility that extend beyond the products they sell.

    24→[**Risk Management**: Integrating ESG factors into corporate governance helps      companies identify and manage risks associated with environmental and social issues. This proactive approach can prevent potential crises and enhance long-term stability. ](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=452bc2afa552f17689a15b3651828b94961454816aebbbe8c144d5d50e9ad59fJmltdHM9MTc2OTgxNzYwMA&ptn=3&ver=2&hsh=4&fclid=215cdef7-1735-6765-2800-c80416176699&psq=role+of+esg+in+corporates&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly9jb3Jwb3JhdGVmaW5hbmNlaW5zdGl0dXRlLmNvbS9yZXNvdXJjZXMvZXNnL2ltcG9ydGFuY2Utb2YtZXNnLWluLWNvcnBvcmF0ZS1nb3Zlcm5hbmNlLw&ntb=1)
    25→
    26→[**Investor Demand**: There is a growing demand from investors for companies to adopt ESG principles. Investors are increasingly considering ESG factors              alongside traditional financial metrics when making investment decisions.         Companies that demonstrate strong ESG performance are often viewed as    more sustainable and less risky investments. ](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=2a5ff0a89c2d39068f7f1c131d8fc69639d557ab964a6fd21ddf6cae2172912cJmltdHM9MTc2OTgxNzYwMA&ptn=3&ver=2&hsh=4&fclid=215cdef7-1735-6765-2800-c80416176699&psq=role+of+esg+in+corporates&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly9lc2d0aGVyZXBvcnQuY29tL3doYXQtaXMtZXNnLWFuZC13aHktaXMtaXQtaW1wb3J0YW50Lw)

    29→
    30→[**Stakeholder Engagement**: Companies that prioritize ESG are more likely to       engage positively with stakeholders, including employees, customers, and         communities. This engagement can lead to improved brand loyalty, employee   satisfaction, and overall corporate reputation. ](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=2a5ff0a89c2d39068f7f1c131d8fc69639d557ab964a6fd21ddf6cae2172912cJmltdHM9MTc2OTgxNzYwMA&ptn=3&ver=2&hsh=4&fclid=215cdef7-1735-6765-2800-c80416176699&psq=role+of+esg+in+corporates&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly9lc2d0aGVyZXBvcnQuY29tL3doYXQtaXMtZXNnLWFuZC13aHktaXMtaXQtaW1wb3J0YW50Lw&ntb=1)
    31→
    32→A **sustainable business**, or a green business, is an enterprise which has (or aims to have) a minimal negative impact or potentially a positive effect on the global or local [environment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_environment), [community](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Community), society, or economy—a [busi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Business)ness that attempts to meet the [triple bottom line](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Triple_bottom_line). They cluster under different groupings and the whole is sometimes referred to as "[green capitalism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Green_capitalism)". Often, sustainable businesses have progressive environmental and human rights, policies. In general, a business described as green if it matches the following four criteria:
    33→
    34→1. It incorporates principles of sustainability into each of its business decisions.  
    35→2.  It supplies environmentally friendly product or services that replace demand for non-green products and/or services.  
    36→3.  It is greener than traditional competition.  
    37→4.  It has made an enduring commitment to environmental principles in its business operations.
    38→
    39→The Brundtland Report emphasized that sustainability is a three-legged stool of people, planet, and profit. Sustainable businesses within the supply chain try to balance all three using sustainable development and sustainable distribution to affect the environment, business growth, and society.
    40→
    41→A major initiative of sustainable businesses is to eliminate or decrease the environmental harm caused by the production and consumption of their goods. The impact of such human activities in terms of the number of greenhouse gases produced can be measured in units of carbon dioxide and is referred to as the *carbon footprint*. The carbon footprint concept is derived from the ecological footprint analysis, which examines the ecological capacity required to support the consumption of products
    42→
    43→In the environmental sphere, sustainable practices include going paperless, refurbishing used products, *revising production processes* to eliminate waste, up cycling clothes or textiles. In the social sphere, for a business to be truly sustainable, it must sustain not only the necessary environmental resources, but also social resources including employees, customers (the community), and its reputation.
    44→
    45→Green washing refers to make vague or false environmental claims about a product or service they are selling. Green washing also refers to an act of over exaggerating the beneficial effects a product may have on the environment
    46→
    47→**The widely practiced strategies include Innovation, Collaboration, Process Improvement, Sustainability reporting and greening the supply chain.**
    48→
    49→1.  Innovation & Technology: This method focuses on a company's ability to change its products and services towards better environmental impacts, for example less waste production.
    50→
    51→2.  Collaboration: The formation of networks with similar or partner companies facilitates knowledge sharing and propels innovation.
    52→
    53→3.  Process Improvement: Continuous process surveying and improvement are essential to reduction in negative impacts. Employee awareness of company-wide sustainability plan further aids the integration of new and improved processes.
    54→
    55→4.  Sustainability Reporting: Periodic reporting of company performance in relation to goals encourages performance monitoring internally and transparency and accountability externally. The goals might then be incorporated into the corporate mission.
    56→
    57→5.  Greening the Supply Chain: Sustainable procurement is important for any sustainability strategy as a company's impact on the environment is much bigger than the products that they consume. The B Corporation (certification) model is a good example of one that encourages companies to focus on this.
    58→
    59→One critical milestone was the establishment of the ISO 14000 standards whose development came as a result of the Rio Summit on the Environment held in 1992\. This standard is the cornerstone standard of the ISO 14000 series. This specifies a framework of control for an Environmental Management System against which an organization can be certified by a third party.
    60→

1→---
     2→title: "CHALLENGES FOR SUSTAINABILITY"
     3→part: 25
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3793-3819"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : CHALLENGES FOR SUSTAINABILITY**
     9→
    10→**No importance**  
    11→There are many in a corporate who do not care for the environment, society, community and humanity where we live so long as they or their family are not in “visibly” trouble. For example, the lungs of a child or even an adult may be deteriorating due to breathing polluted air but may not be visible. Once he is diagnosed for asthma or COPD, the parents may be “visibly” troubled. There are published articles in the newspapers by scientists that a person living in a polluted city like Delhi has a 9.6 years lesser life span, but many do not care until someone close to them dies of pollution.
    12→
    13→The damage to environment, and therefore to the society, community and humanity, is happening due to many factors. For example, cutting trees, using pesticides in crops, flowing chemical waste to rivers and water bodies, burning leaves, stubble burning, driving polluting cars, bursting banned firecrackers, throwing e-waste, use of banned plastics, etc are damaging the environment significantly. The environment where we live, the air which we breathe, the water which we drink, the food that we eat, everything is polluted.
    14→
    15→Above environmental hazards are leading to health problems and diseases as severe as cancer, not only to human beings but to the flora and fauna of this beautiful earth. And people are ignorant to the fact that we are leaving a dangerous earth to live in for our children of the future. But who cares?   
    16→
    17→**No funds**  
    18→Since sustainability has less importance to many, the funds kept for the same are meager. Rather many business leaders feel that spending for sustainability is a waste and the money spent could have otherwise increased the profit margins.
    19→
    20→**No dedicated team**  
    21→Practicing sustainability is not an easy task. It requires interest, dedication and a consciousness towards environment, society, community and humanity. Often corporates designate someone as the Sustainability Manager of the organisation and the poor chap runs helter-skelter to arrange funds and achieve some of his sustainability targets. A dedicated team needs to drive such activities and the team needs to convince all employees about its impact. Without the participation of all employees in a Sustainability drive, you cannot achieve desired outcomes. Therefore it is clearly not one man’s or one team’s job. The results will be the aggregate of everyone’s effort towards the drive.
    22→
    23→**No recognition and award**  
    24→Now it is clear that if sustainability gains importance among employees, the organisation earmarks sufficient funds to drive the sustainability goals through a dedicated team, and then drivers of this effort can as well be recognised. The problem is that some organisations lay importance only to its people who drive the business and generate revenues and profits but seldom do they think to recognise those who are responsible for creating a green environment while conducting business, so that we and the children of the future can lead a healthy and peaceful life eating, drinking and breathing well.
    25→
    26→**No consciousness**   
    27→Climate Change, Green Energy and Cleanliness have taken immense importance in the governance of a country. A country prospers in these aspects only when organizations realize the importance of these issues in the modern world. Our world of land, air and water is polluted heavily and it is the responsibility of all organisations to pay heed to these issues to make the earth healthily liveable.  
    28→
    29→**The salient points are:**
    30→
    31→1) Importance to implement sustainability measures must be propagated.  
    32→2) Institute an award system to encourage and prize employees for their ESG activities
    33→

    12→
    13→You must be aware of Car Insurance. If you are an owner of a bike or a car, you know that car insurance is mandatory if you own a car or a bike. The insurance can be a third-party insurance covering loss or damage to third parties which is mandatory, while comprehensive insurance which covers all the cars for bumper-to-bumper damage and all the individuals involved in the accident including yourself is optional in India. However, Personal Accident Insurance, known as PA cover, is also mandatory as per law in India.
    14→
    15→We also have house insurance covering loss or damage to house property and its contents, life insurance covering financial benefits to your nominee in case of your death, health insurance covering medical expenses of yourself and your family, EMI insurance covering waiver of EMIs in case of your death, and cybercrime insurance in case you are cheated or extorted by cyber criminals.
    16→	  
    17→You may observe that all the above kinds of insurances are covers to different risks. And the premium is a cost incurred to cover the risk. 
    18→
    19→Similar to above insurances, you can also take a Project insurance covering the losses or damage to a Project due to unforeseen circumstances. Coupled with this, you have to take a Workmen Compensation Policy (WCP) to cover lives of workmen and vehicle insurances for all the vehicles attached to the project or elsewhere. WCP has to be mandatorily taken for any project in India.

    10→**Role of L\&D in a corporate**  
    11→The role of Learning and Development is multifold.
    12→
    13→1) Enhancing skills – identifying skill gaps and customising skill training  
    14→2) Career development – with skill training, employees develop their career  
    15→3) Leadership training – potential employees are given leadership training to become the leaders of the future

    17→5) Compliance training – employees are made aware of the relevant laws and regulations
    18→

    20→
    21→If we don’t know or know what to change but don’t know how to change, then training & learning is the only way to catch up with the latest modern methods. content.text: Let me also check the IT chapter 01 for "high end" / "low end" in the salient points that I may have missed.

content.content:
1→---
     2→title: "LOW-TECH to LOW-LEVEL"
     3→part: 21
     4→section: 4
     5→original_lines: "3519-3534"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : LOW-TECH to LOW-LEVEL**
     9→
    10→Many leaders of the older generation, by virtue of occupying senior positions in corporates, are provided high-end computers, laptops and tablets for their performance. Many such leaders do not know how to use them properly except somehow being able to log in to their mail account. Such trivial jobs could have been performed easily by a low-end laptop but due to seniority, prestige and ego, the IT Head provides them a very costly laptop, underutilised forever.
    11→
    12→And it could be the vice versa. I have seen many junior people handling data-crunching graphics like AutoCAD drawings being given very low-end computers or laptops which struggle to display the complex diagrams on screen for minutes. The poor chap, being junior with no ego, deserves a high-end laptop but cannot get it as per the IT rules of the company. What a misery\!
    13→
    14→What can the IT Head do? Firstly, he should find out the configuration of a system required for different applications. In the above case, the draughtsman working with the AutoCAD software must get a high-end laptop. After all, the company gains, isn’t it? For the higher positions requiring mundane jobs to be performed on the laptop, the boss may opt for a lower-end laptop or no laptop at all (in case he has his personal one) against a financial incentive like a Manager getting car allowance in case he does not opt to take a car from the company even though he is eligible for the same.
    15→
    16→Therefore why not provide incentive to a senior leader for not taking a laptop? The company may incur a much lower cost than providing the laptop. 
    17→
    18→**The salient points are:**
    19→
    20→1. The configuration of a laptop or computer should be the basis of providing the same to the employees based on the type of software the employee uses.  
    21→2. It is pointless to provide high-end laptops to seniors who would use low-end software.  
    22→3. Incentive may be given to those who would not opt for a laptop and use their own. However, due to security reasons, the organisation may not allow employees to use their own laptops.
    23→

     8→**CHAPTER : FALSE CLAIMS**
     9→
    10→Some managers in the Insurance department tend to claim higher amounts for project losses than the actual losses. They feel that the surveyor from the insurance company will find some discrepancies and strike off some portion of their claims, and hence they claim higher. In my opinion, such an act is unethical. And if the claims are genuine, then it is also unethical on the part of the surveyor to strike some portion of the claims. I have observed that employees unethically submit higher claims in fear and anticipation that some portion of the claims will be struck off by the surveyor.  
    11→
    12→Such an unfair practice on the part of the employee as well as on the part of the surveyor is unwarranted. Employees should resort to filing correct claims with proof and surveyors should okay claims wherever they are substantiated with proof.
    13→
    14→**The salient points are:**
    15→
    16→1. Insurance department should not resort to false claims of higher amounts than the actual losses.  
    17→2. Insurance department should convince the surveyors that their claims are genuine and should not be opposed.
    18→
